headline: Graying prison population spikes health care costs, drives ‘budget squeeze’
summary: New Jersey faces a structural deficit in its state budget amid federal funding threats, prompting calls for belt-tightening. Prison healthcare costs have risen 44% to $183.1 million annually despite the prison population falling by half, creating budget pressure as the state negotiates a new contract. The department plans to resume copays that generated only $358,000—0.03% of its $1.2 billion budget—in the last full pre-pandemic year.

headline: Public Religion Research Institute survey finds strong support, sympathy for Christian nationalism
summary: A 2025 Public Religion Research Institute survey of over 22,000 adults found 13% of Americans support Christian nationalism while 64% reject it. The analysis identifies Idaho as a key center for the movement due to pastor Doug Wilson's influential Christ Church network. The survey revealed 56% of Republicans support the ideology, creating disproportionate influence within the party. Trump administration officials like Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth have direct ties to Idaho's Christian nationalist circles, linking the movement to federal governance.

headline: Minnesotans travel to D.C. to press for ICE funding cuts after Operation Metro Surge
summary: Minnesotans impacted by Operation Metro Surge, which killed two residents and arrested thousands, traveled to Washington D.C. to demand congressional action to defund ICE and ban controversial enforcement tactics; Democrats successfully blocked a Department of Homeland Security funding bill that failed to include these demands, though ICE and Border Patrol remain largely operational due to prior funding allocations.

headline: NM Senate committee advances bill to expand firefighter cancer coverage
summary: New Mexico's House Bill 128, which passed the House 61-1 and was unanimously advanced by a Senate committee, would extend workers' compensation coverage to firefighters diagnosed with lung, ovarian, and cervical cancers by presuming these illnesses are work-related. The legislation addresses current disparate requirements where firefighters must prove their cancer is job-caused, with varying service time thresholds for different cancers. Firefighters testified about exposure to countless carcinogens during decades of service, and one firefighter with stage 3 lung cancer noted his oncologist confirmed the link between firefighting and his disease. The bill represents a policy shift recognizing occupational cancer as a public health issue affecting emergency responders.

headline: NM Senate passes $11B budget without pay raise for state employees
summary: The New Mexico Senate passed an $11 billion budget without state worker raises, diverging from the House version that allocated $26 million for 1% increases. All majority Democrats voted for the budget despite criticizing the Senate Finance Committee's elimination of raises, while all Republicans opposed it. Senate Finance Chair George Muñoz defended the cuts as necessary to fund food assistance and elderly care, noting workers received $1.3 billion in raises since 2020. The budget maintains over $3 billion in reserves and continues record spending levels. The House must now vote on whether to accept the Senate changes or enter a conference committee to reconcile differences.

headline: Lawmakers consider $10.5M bill to compensate counties that lose ICE detention centers
summary: New Mexico's Senate Bill 273 proposes $10.5 million over two years to compensate Torrance, Cibola, and Otero counties for revenue losses from House Bill 9's ban on public contracts with ICE for immigrant detention facilities; introduced by Finance Committee Chair Sen. George Muñoz, the bill would offset tax revenue losses, revenue bond payments, and increased prisoner transport costs as a temporary bridge while the state implements its policy to end immigrant detention agreements.

headline: Survey: 1 in 3 Michigan residents align with Christian nationalist views
summary: A 2025 Public Religion Research Institute survey of nearly 700 Michigan residents found 33% qualify as Christian nationalism adherents (11%) or sympathizers (21%), reflecting the state's swing-state status with rates between high-support Southern states (54% in Arkansas) and low-support coastal states (15% in Massachusetts). The poll reveals stark partisan divides: 56% of Republicans nationwide hold these views versus 25% of independents and 17% of Democrats. Conducted online from February to December 2025 with a ±0.87% national margin of error, the survey links Christian nationalism to support for hardline policies including political violence, the "great replacement" theory, and deportation without court challenges. Researchers warn this worldview threatens pluralistic democracy despite being rejected two-to-one nationally, as 73% of adherents support granting President Trump expanded power to "restore America's greatness."

headline: Powerful union federation says ‘hell no’ to ICE ramping up presence in Michigan
summary: ICE's announced withdrawal from Minnesota signals a potential enforcement surge in Michigan, where new detention facilities are emerging in southeast Michigan and the North Lake Correctional Facility in Baldwin has rapidly expanded to approximately 1,500 detainees, most classified as non-criminal. The Michigan AFL-CIO condemned ICE's presence as intimidation and called for complete withdrawal, while data reveals the facility's population grew from under 100 in July 2025 to nearly 1,600 by November 2025 under the Trump administration's mass deportation policies.

headline: ‘It’s obviously unconstitutional.’ Vouchers Hurt Ohio speaks out against new Republican bill
summary: A proposed Ohio law (H.B. 671) threatening to withhold billions in state funding from districts suing over the EdChoice voucher program could spark protests and civil unrest among the 300+ affected school systems, with critics including former Ohio Supreme Court Chief Justice Eric Brown calling the bill "outrageous" and unconstitutional for targeting districts that successfully challenged the voucher program in court.

headline: Lawmaker reverses course on bill to restrict money for Ohio school districts that sue over vouchers
summary: Ohio Rep. Jamie Callender introduced House Bill 671 to restrict state funding for public school districts suing over the state's private school voucher program, initially proposing to place all state aid in escrow until litigation ends. Following widespread backlash, Callender amended the bill to escrow only the amount districts spend on legal fees. The proposal targets approximately 320 districts that sued over a voucher program allowing any family to receive taxpayer dollars for private schools, which a judge ruled unconstitutional but the state is appealing. Callender, who previously championed the Fair School Funding Plan, claims the measure will protect classroom funding, while critics including school board members and former Ohio Supreme Court Justice Eric Brown condemn it as intimidation. Policy Matters Ohio reports K-12 schools face nearly $3 billion in underfunding while private schools received over $1 billion in taxpayer dollars in fiscal year 2025.

headline: Communities fight ICE detention centers, but have few tools to stop them
summary: ICE is pursuing a massive expansion of immigration detention facilities, planning 92,600 beds across new processing sites and detention centers, funded by $45 billion approved by Congress; local governments nationwide are opposing these conversions of warehouses into detention centers due to humanitarian and economic concerns, but face legal limitations under the Constitution's supremacy clause that gives federal law precedence; mayors and officials across party lines have formed a unified position against the facilities while acknowledging their limited legal recourse, turning to public pressure campaigns against private developers and passing local moratoriums or resolutions; some communities have successfully blocked plans through this pressure, though others remain unable to stop federal action, highlighting the tension between local zoning authority and federal immigration enforcement power.

headline: CT school leaders on Lamont’s K-12 budget: ‘A trainwreck for education’
summary: Governor Lamont's budget maintains Connecticut's Education Cost Sharing formula at 2013 levels without inflation adjustment, forcing municipalities to fund school increases through property taxes—Putnam seeks 7.23% to avoid cutting 10% of teachers and all extracurriculars. The unchanged baseline has shifted $820 million in education costs to municipalities since 2013, causing class sizes to approach 30 students, severe shortages of counselors and psychologists, and potential school closures. While Lamont proposes a Blue Ribbon Panel to study reforms, district leaders, superintendents, and teachers unions reject delays and demand immediate ECS increases. If the legislature raises the formula by the estimated $4,500 per student gap, districts could restore specialists, reduce class sizes to optimal 15-20 students, and raise starting teacher salaries from $40,000s to $50,000-$60,000.

headline: Washington state Senate approves tax on personal income over $1M
summary: Washington Senate Democrats advanced a millionaires' tax expected to generate $2.5 billion for the next budget and $3.4 billion annually when fully implemented, though supporters caution it offers little near-term financial relief. Revenue would fund public defense, tax credits, and small business breaks, with surplus going to the general fund for education and health care. A new revenue forecast showing $827 million in additional current collections and over $1 billion in the next biennium has already erased a projected three-year budget shortfall.

headline: Budget committee approves amendments to Knowles-Nelson reauthorization bill
summary: The GOP-controlled Joint Finance Committee advanced a bill to reauthorize the Knowles-Nelson Stewardship program at $28.25 million annually, cutting funding from the current $33 million level, while Democratic proposals ranged from $72 million to over $100 million; the program faces a June 30, 2026 funding deadline, with Republicans citing a state Supreme Court ruling that ended anonymous project objections as necessitating the reduction, and Democrats accusing them of dismantling a bipartisan conservation program out of spite.

headline: Wisconsin, Minnesota officials join march for Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Relatives
summary: Approximately 100 people participated in the 11th annual Memorial March for Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Relatives in Duluth, Minnesota, with participation from Superior, Wisconsin officials including Mayor Jim Paine. The march commemorated the crisis of violence against Indigenous people and called for justice, including in the case of Allison Lussier where the Minneapolis Police Department allegedly failed to investigate. Superior Mayor Paine expressed personal commitment to the issue, referencing the State of The Tribes address at the Wisconsin State Capitol. The event highlighted Minnesota's MMIR office and reward fund, as well as an independent investigation request by the Minneapolis City Council. Marchers carried posters, banners, and symbolic red dresses, concluding at the Building for Women where they released tobacco into a sacred fire.

headline: WV Senate’s budget bill increases tax cut, changes how to pay for $230M school voucher program
summary: The Senate Finance Committee proposed a 10% personal income tax cut costing $250 million in revenue and funding the $230 million Hope Scholarship voucher program entirely through surplus funds rather than general revenue. The budget increases spending for economic development, tourism, higher education, and state employee pay raises while cutting psychiatric facility funding by $35 million. It shifts Medicaid funding to general revenue and raises vape taxes to generate $22 million to offset revenue losses. Democratic lawmakers oppose the tax cut, citing failures to address public education funding crises and childcare needs. Republican senators also expressed concerns about long-term fiscal stability given projections of a $204 million budget gap by fiscal 2028.

headline: Kansas protesters argue for fundamental reform of government in both style and substance
summary: On Presidents Day, five "Handmaid's Tale"-themed protesters inside the Kansas Capitol and hundreds of "Not My President" demonstrators outside protested state and federal policies. The Handmaid group targeted Kansas's SB 244—a vetoed anti-transgender bathroom bill that would impose $1,000 individual bounties and agency fines up to $125,000—and broader concerns about a "theocratic" state government direction. The larger rally, organized by Kansas 50501, focused on opposition to Trump's immigration enforcement and alleged constitutional violations. Both groups expressed frustration with partisan politics prioritizing party over people. The protests highlighted deepening political divisions over LGBTQ+ rights, immigration, and democratic norms in Kansas.

headline: Iowa House panel raises per-pupil school funding proposal to 2.25%
summary: The Iowa House Appropriations Committee approved a 2.25% increase in State Supplemental Aid for K-12 schools, setting per-pupil funding at $8,168 for FY 2027, which exceeds the Senate's 1.75% proposal but remains below the 4.2% revenue growth projection and inflation rate that education advocates say is necessary to avoid program cuts and staffing reductions. The House plan includes a $47.7 million budget guarantee offset to prevent property tax increases, caps transportation equity payments at $1 million per district, and adds $14 million for paraeducator compensation. Democrats and education groups, including the Iowa State Education Association, continue pushing for at least a 5% increase, arguing that insufficient funding will force larger class sizes and reduced course offerings. The amended bill now proceeds to the full House for consideration before returning to the Senate for potential reconciliation.

headline: Iowa hospitals oppose mandatory reporting of major medical errors
summary: Senate File 2185 would require Iowa hospitals, ambulatory surgical centers, and birth centers to report "serious reportable events" to the Department of Inspections, Appeals and Licensing (DIAL). The bill faces opposition from the Iowa Hospital Association, UnityPoint Health, and MercyOne. It mandates root cause analysis and corrective action plans, while explicitly barring punitive investigations by other state agencies. Supporters, including the Iowa Association for Justice, cite estimates of 2,400 annual Iowa deaths from medical errors and argue the bill would improve transparency and quality improvement. However, confidentiality provisions may seal reported information and corrective plans from public view.

headline: Lawmakers question Maine’s health department after federal audit
summary: Maine's Department of Health and Human Services failed to provide effective oversight of $45 million in Medicaid payments for children's autism services, lacking any post-payment review since 2010, which may have compromised accountability for behavioral health care delivery. While state officials agreed to repay $28.7 million to the federal government, they emphasized the payments served legitimate health care needs. The audit highlights critical gaps in monitoring public health program expenditures that could affect service quality and access.

headline: Colorado activists urge owner of empty Hudson prison to nix potential new ICE facility
summary: Clergy and immigrant rights activists demonstrated outside Highlands REIT's Denver properties, demanding the company sever ties with ICE and GEO Group regarding the planned Big Horn Correctional Facility detention center in Hudson. Protesters cited concerns about human dignity amid Trump's mass deportation plans and noted the facility is under a Dec. 1 ICE contract (terms redacted) after a prior $39 million award went unused. Activists plan to canvass tenants at seven Denver apartment buildings to highlight that their rent payments support profits from immigration detention.

headline: Local Lookout: Sheriff gives raises | Students protest ICE | Tax funds fight skeeters
summary: About 200 Cheyenne junior high and high school students walked out Friday to rally at the state Capitol, protesting ICE presence in Wyoming with chants like “ICE Out” and signs referencing active state legislation and agencies that work with ICE.

headline: Freedom Caucus wants UW to change course, setting up budget negotiations at statehouse
summary: Lawmakers are negotiating a two-year state budget featuring a proposed $40 million cut to the University of Wyoming's block grant—an 11% reduction from the governor's recommendation—with carveouts protecting specific programs like Education and Agriculture while denying the governor's requests for $12.5 million in matching funds, $6 million for athletics, and $1.69 million for Wyoming Public Media. The budget imposes new restrictions barring UW from funding Wyoming Public Media, elective abortions, or related health insurance, while directing $6 million to career and technical education and $400,000 to the rodeo team. Freedom Caucus members championed the cuts to force UW to focus on "practical education" in fields like agriculture and engineering, while opponents warn the reductions will increase tuition and drive students out of state. Amendments have been filed to restore funding and remove restrictions, setting up contentious negotiations.

headline: Delaware Republicans press for new “all of the above” as Matt Meyer focuses on renewable energy
summary: Delaware's escalating debate over electricity costs and competing Republican versus gubernatorial energy proposals could generate public discontent potentially leading to protests or civil unrest if rate increases become unaffordable, as legislative disagreements on baseload power versus renewables create political tensions that may mobilize demonstrations.

headline: Montana should say, ‘No thanks,’ to Gov. Gianforte’s crusade
summary: Gov. Gianforte's public endorsement of Charlie Kirk's TPUSA ideology for Montana high schools, promoting submissive gender roles and religious doctrine in public education, could potentially incite protests or civil unrest among students, parents, and community members opposing state-sponsored indoctrination.

headline: Attorney General Paxton launches investigations into three Texas school districts over students protesting ICE
summary: High school students protested outside a suspected ICE detention facility in Pflugerville on Feb. 13, 2026, demonstrating community opposition to immigration enforcement operations. The youth-led action highlights policy tensions regarding federal detention centers in Texas communities. Local officials may face increased pressure to address transparency and oversight of immigration enforcement facilities. The protest reflects growing political mobilization among young people on immigration issues. Such demonstrations could influence state-level policy debates on enforcement practices and community relations.

headline: Health department urges caution after rabid bat bites Strafford resident
summary: A Strafford, Vermont resident is receiving rabies treatment after a bat bite, prompting the Vermont Department of Health and local health officer to issue public warnings about rabies risk. State data reveals a 74% surge in rabies cases in 2024, with 66 animals testing positive, though officials are uncertain about the cause. Vermont law mandates rabies vaccination for cats and dogs, and the Department of Health conducts semi-annual wildlife bait drop vaccination programs to control spread. Public health officials urge residents to report suspicious animals via the Rabies Hotline, immediately wash any bite wounds, and seek medical care, as rabies is nearly always fatal once symptomatic. The incident reflects broader regional concerns, with New Hampshire reporting similar case increases and recent rabid animal attacks in the Upper Valley.

headline: John K. DeBrosse
summary: A memorial service scheduled for April 11, 2026, at The Essex Farmhouse represents a public gathering; the deceased's Parkinson's Disease and associated UVM Legacy Fund may connect to advocacy movements that organize demonstrations for healthcare research funding and policy changes.

headline: North Dakota up to 6 measles cases, with jump in exposure sites in eastern counties
summary: North Dakota's Department of Health and Human Services has confirmed six measles cases in early 2026, with five in Pembina County and one in Williams County, prompting identification of 23 potential exposure sites across eastern cities. The agency is requiring unvaccinated individuals born after 1957 who visited these sites during February 4-11 to quarantine for 21 days, while recommending early vaccination at six months for infants in Pembina County instead of the standard 12 months. State health officials report at least one hospitalization and emphasize that measles is significantly more contagious than influenza, with one infected person capable of transmitting to 18 others. Healthcare systems like Altru Health System have implemented special protocols, requiring symptomatic individuals to call ahead before visiting clinics to prevent waiting room exposures. Four of the six cases occurred in unvaccinated individuals, highlighting that while the MMR vaccine is 97% effective with two doses, the outbreak predominantly affects the unvaccinated population.
